// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 10 — STUDENT USE: FROM BAN TO BOUNDARY

A community college composition instructor spent the fall semester adding "no AI tools" to every assignment sheet. By No

Chapter 10 — Student Use: From Ban to Bo — She did not have the wrong instinct. She had an unworkable tool.
NARRATION

A community college composition instructor spent the fall semester adding "no AI tools" to every assignment sheet. By November she had received three student appeals, discovered that her AI-detection software had flagged a student who wrote in English as a second language, and watched a student who genuinely struggled get zero credit on a paper that was largely their own work. By December she had a policy that generated more conflict than it prevented. She did not have the wrong instinct. She had an unworkable tool.

// SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.

·Name the four policy modes for student AI use and match each to assignment purpose.
·Write student-facing policy language that is specific enough to be teachable.
·Design at least two assignment patterns that make productive AI boundaries visible and learnable.
·Draft a disclosure format that students can use without anxiety.
·Explain to a student why a boundary exists in terms of their own learning.
NARRATION

By the end of this chapter you will be able to: Name the four policy modes for student AI use and match each to assignment purpose. Write student-facing policy language that is specific enough to be teachable. Design at least two assignment patterns that make productive AI boundaries visible and learnable. Draft a disclosure format that students can use without anxiety. Explain to a student why a boundary exists in terms of their own learning.

// SLIDE 03 — WHY BANNING USUALLY FAILS

WHY BANNING USUALLY FAILS.

They rely on detection that does not work reliably.→They do not teach anything.→They do not engage the real question.
NARRATION

Banning generative AI in education is not wrong in principle. There are legitimate tasks , building foundational skills, completing in-class work, high-stakes credentialing , where no-AI is a reasonable and enforceable rule. The problem is using a ban as a universal default. They rely on detection that does not work reliably. AI-detection tools generate false positives at rates that are professionally and legally consequential (U.S. Department of Education, 2023). Writers who are not native speakers of English are disproportionately flagged.

// SLIDE 04 — CORE CONCEPT: FOUR POLICY MODE

CORE CONCEPT: FOUR POLICY MODES

Mode 1 — Prohibited→Mode 2 — Permitted with Disclosure→Mode 3 — Required for Process→Mode 4 — Reflective Use
NARRATION

The research on student AI policy suggests that durable, teachable policies distinguish among different types of use, rather than treating all AI use as identical (UNESCO, 2023; U.S. Department of Education, 2023). This chapter uses four policy modes. Every assignment in a course falls into one of them. Mode 1 , Prohibited No AI tools, full stop.

// SLIDE 05 — WRITING POLICY LANGUAGE THAT W

WRITING POLICY LANGUAGE THAT WORKS.

·What is prohibited (submitting AI-generated text as their own)
·What is permitted (brainstorming, background research, grammar check)
·What is required when they do use AI (a brief note)
NARRATION

Vague policy language generates confusion and conflict. Specific policy language is teachable and fair. The difference is the level at which the rule is written. Too vague: "AI use is not permitted in this course except where appropriate."

// SLIDE 06 — WORKED WALKTHROUGH: REDESIGNIN

WORKED WALKTHROUGH: REDESIGNING THE VULNERABLE ASSIGNMENT

The composition instructor from the opening scene taught a research-based argument essay. Her students were supposed to

Worked Walkthrough: Redesigning the Vuln — She did not need to ban AI. She needed to redesign the work so that AI assistance is visible, bounded, and insufficient
NARRATION

The composition instructor from the opening scene taught a research-based argument essay. Her students were supposed to practice finding sources, evaluating evidence, and constructing an argument. The original assignment asked students to write a 1,200-word essay on a policy topic of their choice. AI could easily produce that essay, beginning to end. She did not need to ban AI. She needed to redesign the work so that AI assistance is visible, bounded, and insufficient to complete the assignment without student thinking.

// SLIDE 07 — THE HUMAN GATE: EXPLAINING THE

THE HUMAN GATE: EXPLAINING THE BOUNDARY TO STUDENTS

Students deserve to know why the boundary is where it is. "Because I said so" or "because it's cheating" are not answers

The Human Gate: Explaining the Boundary — "The reason you can't use AI to write this essay is not that I can detect it. I can't, reliably. The reason is that the
NARRATION

Students deserve to know why the boundary is where it is. "Because I said so" or "because it's cheating" are not answers that build AI judgment. An answer that builds judgment sounds like this: "The reason you can't use AI to write this essay is not that I can detect it. I can't, reliably. The reason is that the goal of this assignment is for you to practice constructing an argument from evidence. If AI does that work, you don't practice it.

// SLIDE 08 — SAMPLE DISCLOSURE FORMAT

SAMPLE DISCLOSURE FORMAT.

Give students a simple format and they will use it. A disclosure requirement that is vague or burdensome will be ignored

Sample Disclosure Format — AI Use Disclosure Assignment: Submitted by: I used the following AI tools in completing this assignment: - Tool: - Stage
NARRATION

Give students a simple format and they will use it. A disclosure requirement that is vague or burdensome will be ignored or gamed. AI Use Disclosure Assignment: Submitted by: I used the following AI tools in completing this assignment: - Tool: - Stage of work: - What I changed: I did not use AI for:

// SLIDE 09 — EQUITY AND ACCESS

EQUITY AND ACCESS

Any AI policy must reckon with the fact that students do not have equal access to AI tools or equal background in using

Equity and Access — Two principles follow from this.
NARRATION

Any AI policy must reckon with the fact that students do not have equal access to AI tools or equal background in using them effectively. A policy that assumes all students have paid accounts, high-bandwidth internet, and prior AI experience will disadvantage students who do not , students who are often already disadvantaged in other ways. First, if you require AI use (Mode 3 or Mode 4), ensure equitable access.

// SLIDE 10 — COMMON MISTAKES

COMMON MISTAKES

Mistake 1: Writing a policy, not a pedagogy.→Mistake 2: Using the same mode for every assignment.→Mistake 3: Relying on detection.→Mistake 4: Treating disclosure as punishment.→Mistake 5: Ignoring the equity dimension.
NARRATION

Mistake 1: Writing a policy, not a pedagogy. A rule without explanation is a rule without traction. The policy becomes teachable when it is connected to a learning outcome the student can see. Always say why. Mistake 2: Using the same mode for every assignment. Different assignments have different intellectual purposes. A timed in-class journal (Mode 1) and a semester-long research project (Mode 2) are not the same thing and should not carry the same AI policy. Match mode to purpose.

// SLIDE 11 — BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 11

BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 11.

You have now addressed the two most structurally important institutional questions: what data should not go into Claude,

NARRATION

You have now addressed the two most structurally important institutional questions: what data should not go into Claude, and what student use looks like when it is designed rather than banned. The next chapter steps back from the classroom to the faculty workday , the writing, advising, research, and administrative work that teaching professionals do alongside their teaching. Claude can assist there too, with the same discipline: human judgment, output verification, and awareness of where the work must remain yours.
